story:
I’m Mario. Twenty years at the Greenleaf Gentlemen’s Club off the 605, a little dive that glows like a dirty, broken tooth. I’m the guy who fixes what breaks and throws out what shouldn’t be there—bouncer, DJ, cook, manager. I know the place like a husband knows a wife he stops looking at. After two decades, nothing surprises me.

Except her.

Call her Sapphire. She walked in like she owned the carpet, the stink, the despair. Climbed on stage at auditions and moved like gravity reported to her. The girls hated her. The customers worshipped her. Me? I was curious. She didn’t flirt, didn’t linger, didn’t leave lipstick on a glass. She danced, cashed out, vanished. A woman without fingerprints.

That should’ve warned me.

Money goes missing on a Tuesday. Thousands—tills, tips, and the office safe that fed the ATM when it ran low. We count up at closing, and the house comes up short. In a place like ours, you don’t call the cops unless the building’s on fire. But this was too big. Rico, the owner who hasn’t walked in since the smoking ban, starts breathing down my neck.

The whispers find their mark fast. New girl, mysterious girl—Sapphire. We search her bag, her locker, her car. Nothing. But suspicion doesn’t need proof. It just needs a spotlight, and there she was, right under it. She swears she didn’t touch a dime. Looks at me like I’m the lifeline and says, You believe me, right? I tell her yeah. I even mean it, or I think I do.

But memory shifts. That’s the thing no one tells you. A week later, a hundred here, two hundred there—always on her shift. I swear I see her slip something into her purse. Maybe it’s lipstick. Maybe it’s cash. My mind fills in the blanks to match the story everyone’s already telling.

The cops come. She cries in the back office while they cuff her. I pat the air with soft words like I’m doing her a kindness. At trial the jury hears “stripper” and sees glitter and single mom and decides the rest. They unearth a teenage shoplifting charge and call it a pattern. The girls don’t show. The customers don’t care. I testify to what I saw—or what I thought I saw, which, under oath, sounds the same. She gets three years. Maybe two, good behavior.

Here’s the part where I’m supposed to say I couldn’t sleep. Truth? I slept fine.

Because it wasn’t Sapphire.

It was me.

I’d been skimming for years. Little bites. Enough to pay debts, stack a cushion. Everyone skims a little in a place like ours; that’s the lie I fed myself until it grew fat. One night there’s a pile of cash and my hand just moves. Later I opened the safe and pulled the ATM refill envelopes too, making the loss big enough to need a scapegoat. Sapphire was a gift—a magician’s distraction. All eyes on her while I worked the trick. I didn’t plant cash on her. I just let the story breathe. A word here, a look there, a clean shirt and steady voice on the stand. It was enough to ruin her, and that’s the part no metaphor fixes.

Now? Maybe I’m on a deck chair in the Mexican Riviera, salt on my lips and a drink sweating in my hand. The club keeps rotting under freeway lights. Rico still rants. Sapphire counts days in a cell.

Or maybe none of that’s true. Maybe I never left. Maybe she was guilty and I just like polishing lies until they shine. I’ve seen it all. What I tell you I’ve seen—that’s another story.
